affordances.name: Uncertainty-type tool routing before analysis
affordances.mechanism: Separate irreducible aleatory variability from reducible epistemic ignorance before choosing the method: route variability to probabilistic sizing, buffers, reference classes, simulations, or scenario analysis, and route ignorance to focused investigation, hypothesis testing, diligence, experimentation, or model revision.

use_when: The problem contains both measurable variability and unresolved ignorance, and the next analytic move depends on which type is present.
One branch needs probabilistic sizing, buffers, simulation, or scenario analysis while another branch needs discovery, diligence, experimentation, hypothesis testing, or model revision.
A team is about to treat all uncertainty as one thing even though different tools are required for reducible and irreducible unknowns.
do_not_use_when: Classifying uncertainty would not change the tool choice, commitment size, or monitoring rule.
The future is reasonably predictable, the cost of error is low, and exhaustive uncertainty classification would add cognitive cost without changing action.
The live task is only calibrating an already identified forecast, not separating irreducible variability from reducible ignorance.
case_evidence_needed: The claim, forecast, decision, or plan element whose uncertainty must be classified.
Which uncertainty is irreducible variability despite better data and which uncertainty is reducible ignorance from missing facts or assumptions.
The analysis tool proposed for each uncertainty type and why that tool matches aleatory risk or epistemic gaps.
The decision, commitment size, or monitoring rule that will change after the split.

treatment_requirements.description: Before analysis, map the specific unknowns by reducibility so irreducible variability is not treated like missing knowledge and reducible ignorance is not treated like unavoidable chance.
evidence_required: The specific uncertain factors, assumptions, events, or outcomes in the case.
Evidence that an item is inherent probabilistic variability, such as chance, complexity, or a distribution of possible outcomes.
Evidence that an item is missing knowledge that could be reduced through investigation, data gathering, or assumption testing.
A statement of why the distinction matters to the decision at hand.
good_output_shape: Lists the case's uncertain items under aleatory variability or epistemic ignorance instead of using one generic uncertainty bucket.
States which items are irreducible enough to size probabilistically and which are reducible enough to investigate.
Keeps unknown unknowns and missing assumptions visible as epistemic gaps rather than burying them in a forecast range.
Explains how the classification changes the next analytic move.

treatment_requirements.description: Assign a fitting method to each uncertainty type: probabilistic sizing, buffers, simulations, scenarios, or reference classes for aleatory risk, and discovery work, diligence, experimentation, hypothesis testing, or model revision for epistemic ignorance.
evidence_required: The aleatory items that need probability, range, reference-class, simulation, buffer, or scenario treatment.
The epistemic items that need focused investigation, valid data, hypothesis testing, five-whys diagnosis, first-principles review, diligence, experimentation, or model revision.
The tool choice that would be wrong if the uncertainty type were misclassified.
good_output_shape: Pairs each uncertainty type with a concrete analysis method.
Rejects more research as the answer to irreducible variability when probabilistic sizing is needed.
Rejects pure probability language for gaps that could be reduced by investigation or assumption testing.
Shows the decision-maker what work should happen next for each uncertainty branch.

diagnostic_questions: Which parts of this uncertainty remain variable even with better data, and which parts could be reduced by focused investigation?
Which branch needs probabilistic sizing, buffers, or simulation, and which branch needs discovery, diligence, experimentation, or model revision?
What tool choice would change if this item were classified as aleatory rather than epistemic?
misuse_guards: Do not compress all uncertainty into one confidence level; preserve irreducible variability versus reducible ignorance.
Do not spend time labeling uncertainty types unless the labels change tool choice, commitment size, or monitoring.
Do not attack aleatory variation with more research alone, or treat epistemic ignorance as unavoidable chance before testing assumptions.
Do not widen this into generic uncertainty navigation; the model earns weight only when the reducible-versus-irreducible split changes the work.

source_evidence.source_quote: Aleatory Epistemic Uncertainty Recognition (AEUR) is the crucial mental model centered on **consciously distinguishing between inherent, irreducible randomness (Aleatory Uncertainty or Risk) and reducible gaps in knowledge (Epistemic Uncertainty or Ignorance)**.
different forms of decision-making and analysis are required for each type

Aleatory uncertainty relates to factors of chance, complexity, and inherent probabilistic variability that are not entirely predictable, even with comprehensive data. This level of uncertainty typically requires thinking probabilistically.
which can potentially be uncovered and resolved through focused investigation and gathering of valid data.
Works when one branch of the problem needs probabilistic sizing, buffers, or simulation while another branch needs discovery work, diligence, experimentation, or model revision.
Danger when the team spends time labeling uncertainty types without changing the actual choice of tool, commitment size, or monitoring rule.

review_notes.normalization_note: Kept the core AEUR operation as a routing contract: classify uncertainty by reducibility before selecting analysis tools. This preserves the source's aleatory risk versus epistemic ignorance distinction instead of turning the model into broad uncertainty language.
dropped_material: Did not split hypothesis testing, five whys, first principles, diligence, experimentation, simulations, buffers, and scenario analysis into separate affordances; in this record they are methods selected after the uncertainty-type split.
Did not promote outside-view calibration as a standalone AEUR affordance because it supports probabilistic sizing of aleatory risk rather than creating an independent reducibility distinction.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm that simulation and scenario analysis remain method choices inside the type-routing affordance unless a later source gives them an AEUR-specific independent contract.

affordances.name: Commitment gating by uncertainty reducibility
affordances.mechanism: Link scale, probe, hedge, wait, and monitoring choices to the uncertainty split: reduce testable epistemic ignorance before irreversible scale, size irreducible aleatory exposure with ranges or worst-case checks, and keep commitment proportional to what can and cannot be learned first.

use_when: The organization must choose whether to scale, probe, hedge, or wait based on how much uncertainty is reducible versus irreducible.
The decision is high-stakes, irreversible, or exposed to catastrophic failure, so commitment size must stay linked to the uncertainty type.
A single-path plan is continuing despite unresolved unknowns that should trigger uncertainty-aware contingencies.
do_not_use_when: The output would only add caveats or ranges without changing commitment size, reversibility, contingency design, or monitoring.
The commitment is low-cost and reversible enough that a full reducibility gate would be unnecessary overkill.
The problem is an explicit safety-factor design task where the output must be a buffer, reserve, or operating margin rather than an uncertainty-type commitment gate.
case_evidence_needed: The contemplated commitment, its scale, and whether it is reversible or exposed to catastrophic downside.
Which uncertainties can be reduced before scaling and which irreducible risks must be sized, hedged, buffered, or monitored.
The assumptions that must be true for the commitment to deserve scale.
The worst-case range, early failure signal, or monitoring rule that changes scale, hedge, wait, or probe decisions.

treatment_requirements.description: Choose scale, probe, hedge, or wait by separating uncertainty that can be reduced before commitment from variability that must be accepted, sized, hedged, or monitored.
evidence_required: The commitment options, including scale, probe, hedge, wait, or a flexible no-regrets move.
The reducible epistemic gaps that should be investigated before irreversible scale.
The irreducible aleatory variability that remains after reasonable investigation.
The reversibility, downside, and timing constraints that determine how much commitment is acceptable.
good_output_shape: States whether to scale, probe, hedge, or wait and ties that choice to reducible versus irreducible uncertainty.
Requires learning before scale when unresolved epistemic gaps would materially change the decision.
Allows commitment under aleatory variability only after exposure is sized through ranges, scenarios, buffers, or hedges.
Names what would change the commitment size or timing.

treatment_requirements.description: For irreversible or catastrophic commitments, expose the assumptions behind the thesis and test the epistemic gaps before treating the plan as ready to scale.
evidence_required: The thesis, forecast, or recommendation that would justify the commitment.
The implicit assumptions and implications that must be true for the thesis to hold.
The data, investigation, sensitivity analysis, or disconfirming evidence check that can make hidden assumptions visible and testable.
The decision reversal, scope reduction, or probe design triggered if those assumptions fail.
good_output_shape: Asks what would have to be believed before commitment and lists those beliefs explicitly.
Turns hidden assumptions into testable epistemic gaps.
Requires sensitivity or disconfirmation checks before irreversible scale.
Reduces, delays, or redesigns the commitment when the core assumptions remain untested.

treatment_requirements.description: For irreducible aleatory exposure, quantify ranges or likelihoods, test the downside boundary, and define an early monitoring signal rather than relying on a single forecast or post-hoc outcome judgment.
evidence_required: The aleatory risk or distribution of outcomes that remains after reducible ignorance is addressed.
The upper and lower range, likelihood, reference class, or scenario evidence used to size exposure.
The worst-case consequence and whether it is severe enough to change the commitment.
The key output or early signal that will detect failure before losses compound.
good_output_shape: Uses ranges, likelihoods, reference classes, simulations, or scenarios to size irreducible variability.
Checks whether the worst case is bad enough to shrink, hedge, or avoid the commitment.
Defines a monitoring rule that changes action while the decision is still reversible enough to matter.
Separates decision quality from aleatory luck when later outcomes are reviewed.

diagnostic_questions: Given what is reducible versus irreducible here, should we scale, probe, hedge, or wait?
What would we have to believe before turning this into an irreversible commitment?
Is the worst case bad enough to require a smaller, reversible, hedged, or monitored move?
What key output can we measure now to detect failure early?
misuse_guards: Do not let uncertainty labels become theater; the split must change commitment size, reversibility, contingency design, or monitoring.
Do not persist with a single-path plan when unresolved unknowns require uncertainty-aware contingencies.
Do not seek certainty before every action; use probe, hedge, wait, or scaled commitment according to what can and cannot be reduced first.
Do not judge the original decision only by the eventual result when aleatory luck can produce bad outcomes from well-structured decisions.

source_evidence.source_quote: Best when confidence, commitment, and reversibility need to stay linked
Best when the organization must decide whether to scale, probe, hedge, or wait based on how much uncertainty is reducible versus irreducible.

High-Stakes Decision Making:** It is essential when decisions are irreversible or catastrophic failure is possible, forcing a move beyond simple pro/con lists to more rigorous tools like cost-benefit analysis or decision trees.

Ask the core question of **"What would you have to believe?"** to accept a particular thesis, spelling out all implicit assumptions and implications. This practice makes "hidden assumptions" visible and testable.
Is the worst case bad enough?
What key output can I measure now to detect failure early?
aleatory-epistemic-uncertainty-recognition conflicts with commitment-bias (commitment bias) when teams persist with single-path plans despite unresolved unknowns.

Outcome bias judges a decision solely by its eventual result. This conflicts with the AEUR philosophy that a "good decision" (one consistent with preferences and complete information) may still lead to a "bad outcome" through bad luck (aleatory failure).

review_notes.normalization_note: Separated commitment gating from tool routing because the source gives an explicit action surface: scale, probe, hedge, or wait based on reducible versus irreducible uncertainty. This is not generic confidence calibration; it requires the uncertainty-type split to change commitment, reversibility, and monitoring.
dropped_material: Did not create a standalone premortem affordance; worst-case and early-output questions serve this commitment gate.
Did not create a standalone margin-of-safety or risk-assessment affordance; buffers and downside checks matter here only when they follow the reducible-versus-irreducible split.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with confidence-calibration: this affordance should activate when commitment depends on uncertainty reducibility, not when a forecast merely needs an earned confidence range.

absence_records.reason: A broad uncertainty-navigation affordance would erase the source's central distinction. AEUR only earns runtime weight when the record preserves different treatment for irreducible aleatory variability and reducible epistemic ignorance.

source_evidence.source_quote: not all uncertainty is the same
different forms of decision-making and analysis are required for each type
Danger when the team spends time labeling uncertainty types without changing the actual choice of tool, commitment size, or monitoring rule.

absence_records.reason: The source supports ranges, overprecision checks, the shock test, outside view, and sensitivity analysis, but in AEUR those methods serve uncertainty-type routing or commitment gating. A separate confidence-calibration affordance would duplicate the existing confidence-calibration model and blur the aleatory/epistemic boundary.

source_evidence.source_quote: The Illusion of Knowing (Overprecision)
Use the **Shock Test**: "Would you be really shocked if the outcome landed at the extreme high or extreme low boundary of my estimate?".
Run **sensitivity analysis** to expose the impact of Epistemic uncertainty that hides in forecasts or estimates. This means moving beyond overconfidence in the precision of estimates.

Aleatory Epistemic Uncertainty Recognition (AEUR) is the crucial mental model centered on **consciously distinguishing between inherent, irreducible randomness (Aleatory Uncertainty or Risk) and reducible gaps in knowledge (Epistemic Uncertainty or Ignorance)**.
Works when one branch of the problem needs probabilistic sizing, buffers, or simulation while another branch needs discovery work, diligence, experimentation, or model revision.
Best when the organization must decide whether to scale, probe, hedge, or wait based on how much uncertainty is reducible versus irreducible.
Danger when the team spends time labeling uncertainty types without changing the actual choice of tool, commitment size, or monitoring rule.

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the extraction contract, schema, PR 9 Batch 2 selection note, full AEUR source, Wave 1 activation curation, Wave 2 intervention semantics, and Wave 3 relation semantics. Extracted two affordances: uncertainty-type tool routing and commitment gating by reducibility. The record preserves reducible epistemic ignorance versus irreducible aleatory variability and avoids generic uncertainty-navigation or confidence-calibration language.
dropped_material: Did not create a third affordance for outside view, scenario planning, shock testing, sensitivity analysis, knowns-and-unknowns matrix, or premortem questions because each functions as a method inside tool routing or commitment gating.
Did not promote relation material for probabilistic thinking, systems thinking, falsifiability, first principles, confirmation bias, WYSIATI, or outcome bias into separate AEUR affordances.

Did not duplicate confidence-calibration, margin-of-safety, risk-assessment, experimentation, or base-rates records; AEUR contributes the uncertainty-type split that chooses among those adjacent treatments.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm whether two affordances are still sufficiently sparse for this long source; my judgment is that adding a third would merge method examples into a generic duplicate.
Reviewer should check runtime retrieval boundaries so AEUR fires only when reducibility classification changes the method, commitment, or monitoring choice.
